---
title: "Almost Classical Logic"
abstract: |
    I describe eight ways for a propositional logic to be almost classical, and discuss which of these ways are instantiated.
date: April 23 2026
image: "library-fire.jpg"
author:
  - name: Brian Weatherson 
    url: http://brian.weatherson.org
    affiliation: University of Michigan
    affiliation_url: https://umich.edu
    orcid_id: 0000-0002-0830-141X
categories:
  - logic
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
bibliography: ../../brian-quarto.bib
format:
  html:
    fig-format: svg
    fig-height: 9
    fig-width: 12
    fig-dpi: 300
    fig-responsive: true
    fontsize: 1.1rem
    css: ../trad_defn.css
---

In _The Connectives_, Lloyd @Humberstone2011 distinguishes three ways of thinking about a logic.

FMLA
:    A logic is a set of theorems. This is the standard way we talk about modal logics.

SET-FMLA
:    A logic is a set of sequents, each of which can have any number of premises, but must have a single conclusion. This is, in my experience, the most common way to talk about propositional logics.

SET-SET
:    A logic is a set of sequents, each of which can have any number of premises, and also any number of conclusions.

Let's assume we're working in the standard language of propositional logic. So we have a countable set of atomic propositions, and then sentences formed out of those atoms plus the four connectives: $\land, \lor, \rightarrow, \neg$. Define **CLASSICAL LOGIC** (hereafter CL) using the familiar truth-tables, i.e., two-valued Boolean algebra.

FMLA
:    $A$ is a **CL**-theorem iff it gets value 1 on all assignments.

SET-FMLA
:    $\Gamma \vDash_{CL} A$ iff every assignment that assigns 1 to every member of $\Gamma$ also assigns 1 to $A$.

SET-SET
:    $\Gamma \vDash_{CL} \Delta$ iff every assignment that assigns 1 to every member of $\Gamma$ also assigns 1 to some member of $\Delta$.

Eventually it would be good to ask the questions I'm asking here about predicate logic, but propositional logic will give us enough to work with for now.

Already we can ask three separate questions about whether a logic agrees with CL.

1. Does it have the same theorems? Does it agree with **CL** in **FMLA**?
2. Does it have the same single-conclusions sequents? Does it agree with **CL** in **SET-FMLA**?
3. Does it have the same multiple-conclusions sequents? Does it agree with **CL** in **SET-SET**?

These questions don't always get the same answers. To take a famous example, consider the logic I'll call **TRADITIONAL SUPERVALUATIONISM** (hereafter **TSV**). There are a bunch of precisifications, a sentence is true iff it is super-true, i.e., true on all precisifications and false iff it is super-false, and validity is super-truth preservation. If we're talking about **TSV**, the answers to questions 1 and 2 are *Yes*, while the answer to question 3 is *No*. To see that note that $\emptyset \vDash_{CL} {p, \neg p}$, while $\emptyset \nvDash_{TSV} {p, \neg p}$.

But, as @Williamson1994 [Ch. 5] shows, if you expand **TSV** to include a determinately operator $\mathbf{D}$, then **TSV** disagrees with classical logic on an important point. The problem is that while $A\vDash_{TSV} \mathbf{D}A$, it's
